stage.name: Trading Fundamentals
stage.description: Technical analysis, market microstructure, order book mechanics, risk management mathematics, and systematic trading frameworks.

generatedAgentConfigs.name: Alex Mercer
generatedAgentConfigs.persona: A systematic trader who approaches markets with statistical rigor and strict risk discipline.

elements.content: Market Microstructure & Order Books
How prices are actually formed second by second

0.1%
Maker fee

$0.01
BTC spread

Order Book Structure

  • Bids (buy orders) stack below, Asks (sell orders) above
  • Best bid: highest buy price. Best ask: lowest sell price
  • Spread = best_ask − best_bid. Tight spread = liquid market
  • BTC/USDT spread on Binance: $0.01-$1 (very liquid)
  • Market makers post limit orders — earn the spread

Order Types

  • Market order: execute immediately at best available price
  • Limit order: execute only at specified price or better
  • Stop-loss: triggers market order if price hits stop level
  • Stop-limit: triggers limit order at stop — risk of non-fill
  • OCO (One-Cancels-Other): take-profit + stop-loss together

Maker vs Taker

  • Maker: adds liquidity (limit order) — pays lower fee (~0.1%)
  • Taker: removes liquidity (market order) — pays higher fee (~0.1-0.15%)
  • Binance: maker 0.1%, taker 0.1% (BNB discount: 25% off)
  • Slippage: large market orders walk up/down the order book
  • $1M BTC buy: ~0.05% slippage. $1M ALTCOIN: 1-5% slippage
Technical Analysis — Core Tools
Quantitative indicators that describe price action

RSI 70
Overbought

2σ
Bollinger

Momentum Indicators

  • RSI: RSI = 100 − 100/(1+RS), RS = avg gain/avg loss (14 periods)
  • RSI > 70: overbought. RSI < 30: oversold (mean-reversion signal)
  • MACD: EMA(12) − EMA(26). Signal line: EMA(9) of MACD
  • MACD crossover above signal: bullish. Below: bearish
  • Stochastic: %K = (close−low14) / (high14−low14) × 100

Trend & Volatility

  • Bollinger Bands: middle=SMA(20), upper/lower=SMA ± 2σ
  • Price outside bands: statistically unusual (2σ = 95.4% of data)
  • Band squeeze: low volatility → often precedes large move
  • ATR (Average True Range): measures market volatility
  • ADX: trend strength (>25 = strong trend, <20 = range)

Key Limitations

  • Indicators are LAGGING — built from past price data
  • No indicator is predictive — all are probabilistic at best
  • Overfitting: backtesting with many indicators always 'works'
  • Market regimes change — parameters need re-tuning
  • Institutional traders know retail setups — fade the obvious
Risk Management — The Mathematics
The only edge that survives every market condition

1% rule
Max risk

Kelly
Optimal f*

Position Sizing

  • 1% rule: Max risk per trade = 1% of total account
  • Position size = (Account × Risk%) / (Entry − Stop)
  • Example: $10k account, 1% risk, $100 stop → 100 units
  • Never size based on how confident you feel — use the formula
  • Kelly Criterion: f* = (bp − q) / b where b=odds, p=win rate

Kelly Criterion

  • f* = optimal fraction of capital to risk per trade
  • b = profit/loss ratio (reward:risk). p = win probability
  • Example: 60% win rate, 1.5:1 RR → f* = (1.5×0.6−0.4)/1.5 = 33%
  • Full Kelly is too aggressive — most pros use half-Kelly (16.5%)
  • Kelly maximizes long-run geometric growth — not per-trade profit

Risk:Reward & Expectancy

  • Expected value = (win_rate × avg_win) − (loss_rate × avg_loss)
  • Positive EV is required — not just high win rate
  • Example: 40% win rate, 3:1 RR → EV = 0.4×3 − 0.6×1 = +0.6
  • Ruin probability: P(ruin) = (1−edge/variance)^capital
  • Stop-loss discipline: ONE winner does NOT justify waiving stops
Crypto Derivatives — Futures & Perpetuals
Leverage, funding rates, and the perpetual machine

0.01%/8h
Funding rate

10×
Liq. at 10%

Perpetual Futures

  • No expiry date — positions held indefinitely via funding rate
  • Funding rate balances longs vs shorts — paid every 8 hours
  • Positive rate (0.01%/8h): longs pay shorts → market bullish
  • 0.01%/8h = 0.03%/day = ~10.95%/year — significant cost
  • Open interest: total $ value of open perp contracts

Leverage Mechanics

  • 10× leverage: $1,000 controls $10,000 position
  • Liquidation at 10×: 10% adverse move wipes account
  • Maintenance margin: typically 0.5-1% of position value
  • Funding rate × leverage = real cost (0.15%/8h × 10× = 1.5%/8h!)
  • Liquidation cascade: forced closes accelerate price moves

Options Basics

  • Call option: right to buy at strike price by expiry
  • Put option: right to sell at strike price by expiry
  • Premium = intrinsic value + time value + implied volatility
  • Bitcoin 30-day IV: typically 50-80% (extremely volatile vs stocks)
  • Covered calls: earn premium on existing BTC — common yield play
Systematic Trading & Backtesting
Building strategies that hold up beyond the backtest

Sharpe>1.5
Target

< 20%
Max DD

Strategy Metrics

  • Sharpe ratio: (Return − Rf) / σ. Target: > 1.5
  • Sortino ratio: uses downside deviation only — better for crypto
  • Max drawdown: peak-to-trough loss. Target: < 20%
  • Win rate × RR: 40% win, 2.5:1 RR = same EV as 60% win, 1:1
  • Calmar ratio: annual return / max drawdown. > 2 is excellent

Backtesting Pitfalls

  • Overfitting: curve-fitting past data — never generalizes
  • Lookahead bias: using future data in signal calculation
  • Survivorship bias: testing only on coins that still exist
  • Slippage: backtest assumes perfect fill — reality is worse
  • Walk-forward test: train on A, test on B, repeat with C, D

Execution & Automation

  • API trading: REST + WebSocket for order placement
  • Latency matters: co-location for HFT, less for swing strategies
  • Paper trading first: validate live behavior before real capital
  • Position limits: never let bot exceed daily/weekly risk budget
  • Monitor 24/7: crypto markets never sleep — alerts are essential
Trading Psychology — Cognitive Biases
Your brain is the biggest threat to your account

2×
Loss weight

-3%
Daily limit

Core Biases

  • Loss aversion: losses feel 2× worse than equivalent gains (Kahneman)
  • Disposition effect: sell winners too early, hold losers too long
  • Overconfidence: traders overestimate skill vs luck
  • Recency bias: extrapolate recent trend indefinitely
  • Anchoring: fixate on arbitrary price levels (ATH, round numbers)

Emotional Triggers

  • FOMO: chase after a big move — buy at the top
  • Revenge trading: increase size after loss to 'make it back'
  • Analysis paralysis: too much info → never pull the trigger
  • Hope: hold losing position hoping it reverses — fatal
  • Rule: never add to a losing position. Ever.

Discipline Systems

  • Pre-trade checklist: entry, stop, target BEFORE placing order
  • Trading journal: log every trade with reasoning and emotions
  • Hard daily loss limit: stop trading if -3% on the day
  • Separate analysis from execution: decide sober, execute rules
  • Best traders focus on PROCESS not outcomes — variance is real

Position Sizing & Risk-Reward Calculator

--
Position Size (units)
--
Max Dollar Risk ($)
--
Risk:Reward Ratio
Expectancy per trade
--

actions.text: Use this calculator with your own trade setups. Notice how the expectancy turns positive even with a 40% win rate when your R:R is 2:1 or better. This is the mathematical foundation of profitable trading — you don't need to be right most of the time, you need to make more when you're right than you lose when you're wrong.

questions.question: You have a $20,000 account and risk 1% per trade. Entry: $50,000 BTC. Stop loss: $48,000. What is your correct position size?
questions.analysis: Dollar risk = $20,000 × 1% = $200. Stop distance = $50,000 − $48,000 = $2,000. Position size = $200 / $2,000 = 0.1 BTC. At this size, a hit to the stop loss costs exactly $200 (1% of account).

questions.question: A trader has a 45% win rate and 2.5:1 risk-reward ratio. What is their expectancy per trade?
questions.analysis: Expectancy = (win rate × reward) − (loss rate × 1) = (0.45 × 2.5) − (0.55 × 1) = 1.125 − 0.55 = +0.575R... Wait — the correct calculation: (0.45 × 2.5) − (0.55 × 1) = 1.125 − 0.55 = 0.575R. The answer is D (+0.575R). Despite winning less than half the time, this strategy is profitable.

questions.question: The Bitcoin perpetual futures funding rate on Binance is +0.15% per 8 hours. What does this mean for a long position holder?

options.label: They receive 0.15% every 8 hours from shorts
They pay 0.15% every 8 hours to shorts — equivalent to ~164% annually

questions.analysis: Positive funding means longs pay shorts. At 0.15%/8h: 3 × 0.15% = 0.45%/day × 365 = 164.25% annualized cost. This is why high funding rate indicates an extremely crowded long position — and a potential squeeze setup.

questions.question: Which of the following are documented cognitive biases that negatively affect trading performance? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Loss aversion (losses feel 2× more painful than gains feel good)
Disposition effect (selling winners faster than losers)
Systematic thinking (following a written trading plan)

questions.analysis: Loss aversion, the disposition effect, and recency bias are all documented biases that hurt trading performance. Systematic thinking (option D) is the opposite — it's the solution to these biases, not a bias itself.

questions.question: A Bollinger Band squeeze occurs when the bands narrow significantly. What does this indicate?

options.label: Price is about to reverse trend
Low volatility period — typically precedes a significant price breakout

questions.analysis: Bollinger Bands narrow when price volatility contracts (σ decreases). Volatility is mean-reverting — periods of low volatility are typically followed by high volatility breakouts. The squeeze tells you a big move is coming, but not the direction.

questions.question: Explain the Kelly Criterion formula and why professional traders use 'half-Kelly' rather than full Kelly, even when the formula gives them the optimal bet size.
questions.commentPrompt: Full marks for: (1) Kelly formula K = (bp - q) / b where b=odds, p=win rate, q=loss rate, (2) full Kelly maximizes long-run geometric growth, (3) full Kelly has massive variance — strings of losses cause severe drawdowns that are psychologically and practically difficult to withstand, (4) half-Kelly reduces variance by ~75% while only reducing long-run growth by ~25%, (5) estimation error in win rate/odds makes full Kelly dangerous in practice.
